subject: ap-chemistry
theme: Chemical reactions are classified by type and involve transfers of mass (stoichiometry), charge (redox), or protons (acid-base), always obeying conservation of atoms and charge.
core_concepts: Reaction types: synthesis, decomposition, single displacement, double displacement, combustion, acid-base (neutralization), and precipitation.
Redox reactions involve electron transfer: oxidation (loss of electrons) and reduction (gain of electrons) always occur together; track via oxidation states.
Net ionic equations omit spectator ions, showing only the species that actually change; must balance atoms AND charges.
Stoichiometry uses mole ratios from balanced equations; limiting reagent determines maximum yield.
Percent yield = (actual yield / theoretical yield) × 100%; values below 100% indicate incomplete reaction, side reactions, or loss of product.
Solution stoichiometry uses molarity ($M = n/V$); titration determines unknown concentrations at the equivalence point.
Precipitation occurs when ion product $Q_{sp} > K_{sp}$; net ionic equation shows the insoluble solid forming.

key_terms.definition: Hypothetical charge on an atom if bonds were fully ionic; sum = charge of species
Species that is oxidized (loses electrons) and causes reduction of another species
Species that is reduced (gains electrons) and causes oxidation of another species
Reactant consumed first that determines the maximum amount of product
Concentration unit: moles of solute per liter of solution ($M = n/V$)
Technique to find unknown concentration using a known-concentration solution until equivalence point
Equation showing only the ions and molecules that change; spectator ions are excluded
Solubility product constant for a slightly soluble salt; product of ion concentrations raised to stoichiometric powers

exam_tip: When balancing redox equations in acidic solution, balance O by adding $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ and balance H by adding $\text{H}^+$; in basic solution, add $\text{OH}^-$ instead.
